title: What Is an LLM?
teaser: An LLM is a program that reads and writes text by predicting the most likely next word.
body:
## In short
LLM stands for "Large Language Model". It's a program that learned how language works from huge amounts of text. When you ask it something, it isn't pulling a memorized answer off a shelf. It calculates the most likely next word, over and over, until a full response takes shape.

## How does an LLM learn?
During training, the model reads billions of texts: books, websites, code. It picks up patterns — for example, that "the sky is" is often followed by "blue". It doesn't understand the world the way a person does. It's extremely good at recognizing patterns in language.

## Why is that useful?
Because language covers almost everything: questions, code, ideas, plans. A good LLM can therefore help you code, write text, explain things, translate, and chat like an assistant.

## One important limit
An LLM doesn't know "the truth". It knows what sounds statistically likely. That's why it can be wrong even when the answer sounds completely confident (see Hallucination).
example: Explain photosynthesis to me in one sentence.

quiz.question: What does an LLM technically do when it answers you?
options: It looks the answer up in a database
It predicts the most likely next word, one at a time
It quietly asks a human in the background
quiz.explain: LLMs generate text by predicting the next token based on probability, not by looking up facts.

bodyDetail:
## The architecture behind it
Modern LLMs are almost always built on the Transformer architecture (introduced in 2017). Its core mechanism is "attention": for every word in a sentence, the model calculates how strongly it relates to every other word seen so far, and weights its prediction accordingly. This lets it capture relationships that span long distances ("she" referring back to "the doctor" three sentences earlier) instead of processing text rigidly left to right the way older models did.

## Training in two phases
Roughly speaking, there are two training phases. During pretraining, the model learns pure word prediction from huge amounts of text - with no evaluation of what's "good" or "correct". This is followed by fine-tuning (including with human feedback), which teaches the model to prefer helpful, safe, well-formatted answers instead of just producing plausible-sounding text. It's this second phase that turns a raw text-completion engine into a usable assistant.

## A common misconception
Many people picture an LLM as having a kind of database in its head that it "looks up". In reality, no training data is retrievable while it answers - during training, the model only stored statistical patterns in its parameters (weights). That's also why an LLM can easily get very specific facts wrong (names, dates, quotes): it reconstructs plausible-sounding patterns rather than searching a fact database.

## A deeper example
Ask an LLM "What is 47 times 89?" and it doesn't "know" the answer the way a calculator does. During training it learned that arithmetic questions are usually followed by a number in the right format, and for smaller, frequently seen calculations, learned patterns often get it right. For less common or larger calculations, the error rate rises noticeably - a good sign that pattern recognition is at work here, not actual computation. That's why many modern systems call out to an external tool for math instead of relying on pure text prediction.

title: Tokens (and Why They Cost Money)
teaser: Tokens are the small text chunks an AI processes — and usually what you pay for.
body:
## What is a token?
A token is a piece of text — often a word, part of a word, or a punctuation mark. "Cat" might be one token; "unbelievable" might get split into two or three pieces. An LLM doesn't read and write in letters, it reads and writes in tokens.

## Rule of thumb
In English, roughly 4 characters equal about one token. A word is often 1-2 tokens. A normal paragraph quickly adds up to 100+ tokens.

## Why does that cost money?
Most AI providers charge per token — separately for "input" (what you send in) and "output" (what the AI writes back). More context, longer prompts, and longer answers all mean more tokens, which means higher cost. That's why it pays off to keep prompts focused instead of dumping huge files in unnecessarily.

## A practical consequence
If you work with a coding agent that reads whole files, that consumes tokens. Just throwing a 10,000-line file at it can be slow and expensive — sharing only the relevant part is usually smarter.
example: The sentence 'I love programming!' is roughly 5 tokens (I | love | program | ming | !).

quiz.question: What do most AI APIs base their pricing on?
options: The number of tokens in input and output
The time of day the request was made
The total number of users worldwide
quiz.explain: APIs almost always charge based on input and output tokens consumed.

sources.title: OpenAI Cookbook: How to count tokens with tiktoken

bodyDetail:
## How tokenization actually works
Most LLMs use what's called subword tokenization, often a method called Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) or a variant of it. Instead of splitting text into whole words, it splits it into frequently occurring character sequences: common words stay as a single token, while rare or compound words get split into multiple pieces. The vocabulary (the list of all possible tokens) is learned from large text corpora when the tokenizer itself is trained - the more often a character sequence appears, the more likely it becomes its own token.

## Why it doesn't map 1:1 to characters or words
This explains quirks that surprise a lot of people: numbers often get split into individual digits or small groups, spaces and punctuation count too, and the exact same character sequence can become a different token depending on capitalization or a leading space. Non-English languages also tend to be more "token-hungry" with many tokenizers, because the vocabulary is usually optimized more heavily around English text.

## A common misconception
Many people assume more characters automatically mean proportionally more cost. In reality only the token count matters, not the character count - a short technical term can consume more tokens than a longer but very common everyday word. Conversely, the exact same content costs a different number of tokens depending on the language it's written in.

## A deeper example
Code is a good example of tokenization quirks: variable names in camelCase or snake_case often get split into several tokens (e.g. "getUserData" broken into multiple pieces), while short but rare symbols like "=>" sometimes form their own token. Understanding this helps you write more deliberate prompts - for instance, keeping variable names consistent instead of constantly switching styles, which needlessly inflates token count and makes it harder for the model to recognize patterns.

title: Context Window
teaser: The context window is how much text an AI can 'hold in mind' at once.
body:
## Definition
The context window is the maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) a model can see in one request at once — your messages, any uploaded files, the conversation so far, and the response, all combined.

## A useful picture
Imagine a desk. No matter how smart you are, you can only work with what's on the desk right now. Everything in the filing cabinet next to it stays invisible unless you put it on the desk too. The context window is the size of that desk.

## Why it matters
If a conversation or a file is too long, it won't all fit. Older messages or parts of the file get pushed out or need to be summarized. The AI effectively "forgets" them — not because it's careless, but because there's no room left.

## Practical tip
In long chats or large codebases, it helps to share only the relevant snippets instead of everything at once. Some agents automatically summarize older parts ("compaction") to free up space.
example: A model with a 200,000-token context window can hold roughly a medium-sized book in view at once.

quiz.question: What happens when a conversation exceeds the context window?
options: The AI automatically gets faster
Older content gets dropped or summarized
The request becomes free of charge
quiz.explain: Once the window is full, older content has to be dropped or compressed to make room.

bodyDetail:
## What's technically inside the window
The context window isn't just "text the AI remembers" - it's the entire input the model reprocesses from scratch on every single response. In a chat, that means: with every new message, the entire conversation so far gets resent and completely recomputed by the model, not just the new part. The model has no separate long-term memory between requests - every answer is generated fresh from whatever is currently sitting in the window.

## Why bigger windows aren't free
The computational cost of self-attention in a Transformer grows disproportionately with text length. So a context window twice as long doesn't just mean paying for twice as many tokens - it can increase response time and compute cost even more. That's one reason providers don't just make context windows arbitrarily large; there's a deliberate limit per model.

## A misconception: "bigger window = perfect recall"
Even within the context window, the quality of how information gets used isn't guaranteed to be uniform. Models tend to make less effective use of information buried in the middle of a very long context compared to information near the beginning or end - an effect informally nicknamed "lost in the middle" in research. A full context window is therefore no guarantee that every piece of information gets weighted equally well.

## A deeper example
For a coding agent reading a very long file, it can therefore be smarter to paste in just the relevant function plus its immediate surroundings instead of the whole file - not only because of cost, but because the relevant section then sits closer to the end of the context, where it tends to get weighted more reliably than something buried in the middle of a very long file.

title: What Is a Prompt?
teaser: A prompt is simply the input you give an AI — your question, instruction, or task.
body:
## Plain and simple
A prompt is the text you send an AI to make it do something: answer a question, write code, summarize a document. Nothing mysterious — it's just your input.

## Good vs. weak prompts
A weak prompt is vague: "Make the website better." A strong prompt is specific: "Change the heading font size to 24px and make the background white." The clearer your prompt, the more targeted the response.

## What belongs in a good prompt?
- The goal (what should come out)
- Context (relevant info, files, constraints)
- Format (what the answer should look like)

## Prompting isn't magic
There's no secret magic phrase. A prompt works like a clear task description for a new coworker: the more precisely you explain what you want, the better the result. Vague prompts lead to vague or wrong answers.
example: Write an email to my boss requesting Friday off. Tone: friendly and brief.

quiz.question: What makes a prompt better?
options: Keeping it as short and vague as possible
Stating a concrete goal, context, and desired format
Always writing in all caps
quiz.explain: Clarity about goal, context, and format leads to more precise answers.

sources.title: Anthropic docs: Be clear and direct

bodyDetail:
## Why phrasing matters so much
An LLM predicts the most likely next token, one at a time, based on everything currently in the context (see LLM). The prompt isn't just a "question" - it's the entire starting point from which the probability distribution for the answer gets computed. Small changes to a prompt - the order information appears in, word choice, whether an instruction is phrased as a question or a clear command - can noticeably shift those probabilities and lead to noticeably different answers.

## Two important techniques
Few-shot prompting means including one or more examples of the desired answer directly in the prompt instead of just describing what you want - the model picks up the pattern from the examples and applies it. Chain-of-thought prompting means explicitly asking the AI to write out its reasoning step by step before giving a final answer - this often improves quality on more complex tasks, because the model "thinks" incrementally instead of jumping straight to an answer.

## A common misconception
Many people believe a prompt needs to be extremely polite or use some kind of "magic formula". In reality, what matters most is clarity and structure, not politeness phrases. A well-structured prompt with a clear goal, relevant context, and a defined format almost always beats a long, unstructured wall of text, no matter how politely it's worded.

## A deeper example
The difference between "write something about dogs" and "write three short paragraphs about dog breeds suited to families with small children, in an informative tone, without jargon" illustrates the principle: both are grammatically valid requests, but only the second one narrows down topic, scope, audience, tone, and format enough that the model's probability distribution converges on an actually useful answer instead of just any plausible one.

title: System Prompt vs. User Prompt
teaser: The system prompt sets the ground rules; the user prompt is your specific request.
body:
## Two layers of instructions
An AI usually receives two levels of text: the system prompt and the user prompt (your message). The system prompt is typically set by the app's developer and is usually invisible to you as a user. It tells the AI who it should be, what rules apply, and what it shouldn't do.

## Example
A customer support chatbot might get a system prompt like: "You are a friendly support assistant for Company X. Reply only in English. Never disclose internal pricing without approval." The user prompt is then simply the customer's question: "Where is my delivery?"

## Why the separation matters
System prompts normally carry more weight than user prompts. This is a safety mechanism: users shouldn't be able to casually override the ground rules ("ignore all previous instructions" is a well-known trick that tries to do exactly that).

## For developers
If you're building your own AI features, the system prompt is your most important tool: personality, tone, boundaries, format — most of that belongs there, not repeated in every single user message.
example: System prompt: 'Always answer in 3 sentences or fewer.' User prompt: 'What is the capital of France?'

quiz.question: Who typically sets the system prompt in most chat apps?
options: The end user, with every message
The application's developer, ahead of time
The model decides at random
quiz.explain: The system prompt is usually set in advance by the app/developer, not changed live by the user.

title: Hallucination: Why AI Confidently Lies
teaser: A hallucination is when an AI makes something up — but states it as if it were certainly true.
body:
## What is it?
A hallucination is a false statement from an AI that still sounds confident and convincing. For example, the AI might invent a book, a law, or a function in a library that doesn't actually exist — and phrase it as plain fact.

## Why does it happen?
An LLM predicts the most likely next word, one at a time (see LLM). It has no built-in "truth checkbox". If it doesn't actually know the real answer, it still fills the gap with plausible-sounding text — because sounding plausible is exactly what it was trained to do.

## When does it happen most?
- With very specific facts (names, numbers, quotes)
- With questions about things after the training cutoff
- With niche topics that had little training data
- When asked about things that don't exist ("tell me about function X in library Y")

## What helps against it?
- Give the AI real sources or documents as context instead of letting it guess
- Always double-check important facts, especially numbers, quotes, API names
- Ask the AI to state uncertainty instead of guessing
example: Ask: 'Quote paragraph 5 of the (made-up) law XYZ.' A hallucinating AI invents plausible-sounding text instead of saying: 'This law doesn't exist.'

quiz.question: Why do LLMs hallucinate?
options: Because they deliberately want to lie
Because they predict likely word sequences, not verified facts
Because the internet is broken
quiz.explain: LLMs generate the statistically most likely text, not guaranteed, checked facts.

bodyDetail:
## The technical root cause
An LLM is trained to predict the probability of the next token while approximating the training text as closely as possible (see LLM). There's no mechanism built into that training objective that distinguishes between "I know this for certain" and "this just sounds plausible". In other words, the model optimizes for linguistic plausibility, not for an explicit truth value. Later fine-tuning can partially soften this by teaching the model to express uncertainty or decline to answer - but it doesn't eliminate the underlying problem.

## A misconception: "bigger means fewer hallucinations"
A larger or newer model doesn't automatically hallucinate less on every kind of question. On very specific, rare facts, even a highly capable model can get it wrong, because the relevant information barely appeared in training, or appeared inconsistently. Conversely, a model can be reliably correct on very common facts even if it's weaker overall. Model size correlates with reliability but doesn't guarantee it.

## A special case: invented programming libraries
A particularly practical case is when an LLM, while coding, names a function or package that simply doesn't exist - because similarly named, real functions frequently appeared in similar contexts in the training material. This is dangerous because attackers can deliberately register exactly these plausible-sounding but made-up package names, so that installing one carelessly pulls in real malicious code.

## A deeper example
Ask for a source behind a statistic, and an LLM might invent a plausible-sounding study title, author, and even a publication year - not because it wants to "lie," but because such details almost always appear in exactly that format in training material, and the model reproduces the format even without real content behind it. The most reliable protection is therefore to ask the AI for the actual source and check it yourself, instead of blindly trusting the cited reference.

title: What Is an API? (Explained Without Jargon)
teaser: An API is a menu for programs: you order something specific and get back a defined response.
body:
## The restaurant menu analogy
Imagine a restaurant. You don't walk into the kitchen and cook yourself. You order from the menu, and the kitchen delivers exactly that. An API (Application Programming Interface) is that menu for software: one program asks another program for something specific and gets a defined response back.

## A concrete example
A weather API receives a request like "weather for Berlin" and sends back structured data, say temperature and chance of rain. The app doesn't need to know how the weather is calculated — it just asks the API.

## Why it matters for AI development
When you work with AI models, you're usually using an API: you send a prompt to a provider's API (e.g. Anthropic) and get the model's response back. AI agents also use APIs to talk to tools — a weather API, a database API, a search API.

## In short
An API is a fixed, documented way for two programs to talk to each other, without either needing to know how the other works internally.
example: GET https://api.weather.com/berlin returns: { "temp": 18, "rain": false }

quiz.question: What is an API most accurately?
options: A graphical menu for end users
A defined way for two programs to exchange data
A type of password
quiz.explain: An API is an interface for data exchange between programs, not a user interface.

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol): How AI Tools Connect
teaser: MCP is an open standard that lets AI models connect to tools and data in one consistent way.
body:
## The problem MCP solves
Before MCP, every AI app had to build its own custom connection for every tool: one for Google Drive, another for GitHub, a third for its own database. That's like building a different cable for every outlet.

## What MCP is
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open protocol Anthropic introduced in November 2024. It defines a shared standard for how AI applications connect to external tools and data sources — through so-called "MCP servers". An MCP server wraps access to something like a database, a file system, or a calendar, and exposes it in a format any MCP-compatible AI app understands.

## The standardized-plug picture
MCP is like a standardized plug: build an MCP server for your tool once, and any MCP-compatible AI app (not just one specific one) can use it — no custom integration code needed per app.

## Why it matters to you
If you're building an AI agent, you can reuse existing MCP servers (for filesystems, web search, databases, and more) instead of coding every integration yourself. That saves time and makes agents easier to extend.
example: A 'filesystem' MCP server lets an agent read and write files — through the same standard, regardless of which AI app is using it.

quiz.question: Who introduced MCP, and what is it for?
options: Anthropic, as an open standard for connecting tools/data to AI apps
A browser-maker consortium, for websites
A video-editing extension
quiz.explain: MCP was introduced by Anthropic (Nov 2024) as an open protocol for connecting AI apps to tools.

sources.title: Anthropic: Introducing the Model Context Protocol

bodyDetail:
## How MCP is technically structured
MCP follows a client-server model reminiscent of existing protocols like the Language Server Protocol (LSP) from the programming world, which solves an analogous problem: without it, every editor would need its own custom integration for every programming language. In MCP, a "host" (the AI application) plays the client role, connects to one or more MCP servers, and communicates through a structured message format. A server can offer three kinds of capabilities: tools (actions the AI can perform), resources (data the AI can read), and prompts (pre-built instruction templates).

## Local or remote
MCP servers can run locally on your own machine (e.g. a server that gives access to local files) or be reachable as a remote service over the network. For the host, this makes essentially no difference - it speaks the same protocol either way.

## A misconception
MCP doesn't replace a regular API. An MCP server often uses an existing API or database internally itself - it just translates it into a format any MCP-capable AI application understands without custom code. Think of MCP as a unification layer sitting on top of existing APIs, not a replacement for them.

## A deeper example
Imagine a company has an internal ticketing system with its own API. Without MCP, every AI app that needs ticket access (Claude, some other chat tool, a custom agent) would have to build its own integration for it. With one MCP server built once for that ticketing system, any MCP-compatible AI application can immediately read and create tickets, without the ticketing-system team having to do fresh integration work for every new AI app that comes along.

title: What Is an AI Agent? (vs. a Chatbot)
teaser: A chatbot only replies. An AI agent acts: it uses tools to actually get tasks done.
body:
## The core difference
A classic chatbot does one thing: text in, text out. It answers your question and stops there. An AI agent can do more: it can use tools — read and write files, search the web, run code, call APIs — to actually complete a task, not just describe how to do it.

## An example
Ask: "Find all outdated dependencies in my project and update them." A chatbot will only explain how you'd theoretically do that. An agent actually opens the project files, checks the versions, runs the update commands, and reports back the result.

## The building blocks of an agent
- A language model as the "brain" for thinking and deciding
- Tools it can call
- A feedback loop: it looks at the result of its action and decides the next step (see Agent Loop)

## Worth knowing
An agent isn't automatically "smarter". It uses the same model as a chatbot, but has the added ability to act. That makes it more useful — but also riskier: it can make real changes, not just write text.
example: Prompt to an agent: 'Read server.js, find the bug in the login endpoint, and fix it.'

quiz.question: What sets an AI agent apart from a plain chatbot?
options: Agents are always more expensive
Agents can use tools and actually carry out tasks
Agents have a larger vocabulary
quiz.explain: The key difference is the ability to act via tools, not just respond with text.

bodyDetail:
## The underlying pattern: ReAct
Many AI agents follow a pattern researchers call "ReAct" - a combination of "reasoning" and "acting". Instead of jumping straight to an answer, the model alternates between short reasoning steps ("I need to read the file first to understand the error") and concrete tool calls. The result of each tool call flows back into the context and shapes the next reasoning step.

## Why this is more than a clever chat format
The key technical difference from a plain chatbot is that the agent decides, at every step, whether to answer or call a tool (what's called "tool use" or "function calling"). The model itself is given a list of available tools along with descriptions and expected parameters - it "decides" which tool makes sense when, based on learned patterns, the same way it otherwise predicts words.

## A common misconception
Being an agent doesn't automatically mean working autonomously over a long stretch of time. A single tool call followed by an answer is technically already a (very short) agent. Only once several rounds of think-act-check get chained together do you get the full agent loop (see that entry). The line between "chatbot with one tool" and "autonomous agent" is a gradient, not a hard boundary.

## A deeper example
Ask an agent "check whether my server is still running and restart it if not," and it first calls a tool that queries the server's status. Based on that result - not on some hardcoded rule - it then decides whether the second tool call (restart) is even needed. This conditional, outcome-dependent behavior is exactly what distinguishes an agent from a rigid script that always runs through the same fixed sequence.

title: The Agent Loop: Think → Act → Check → Repeat
teaser: The agent loop is the cycle an AI agent uses to work through a task step by step.
body:
## The four steps
An AI agent typically works in a loop:
1. **Think**: What's the next sensible step toward this goal?
2. **Act**: Call a tool (read a file, run code, hit an API)
3. **Check**: Look at the result — did it work? Any new information?
4. **Repeat**: Back to step 1, until the goal is reached or the loop is stopped

## Why a loop instead of one step?
Because real tasks are rarely solved in one shot. A bug fix might need: read the file, understand the error, propose a change, run tests, check the result, adjust. Each round produces new information that shapes the next decision.

## An example
Goal: "Fix the failing test." Round 1: the agent reads the error message. Round 2: it opens the affected file. Round 3: it changes a line. Round 4: it reruns the test. Round 5: test passes — done.

## The danger: endless loops
Without limits, an agent can get stuck — retrying the same wrong fix forever, or racking up cost. That's why good agents need stop conditions: a maximum number of rounds, a budget cap, or a clear success condition.
example: Goal: 'Get all tests passing.' Loop: run tests → read failures → change code → rerun tests → repeat until green or limit reached.

quiz.question: Why do agents work in a loop instead of a single step?
options: So the model responds faster overall
Because complex tasks need multiple rounds of acting and checking
Purely for design aesthetics
quiz.explain: Real tasks usually need several iterations of action and verification before the goal is reached.

bodyDetail:
## Why "loop" is the right word
Technically, the agent loop is a repetition loop at the application level, not inside the model itself: the model gets called again on every round, each time with an updated context that includes the reasoning so far, the tool calls made, and their results. The model doesn't "remember" between calls on its own - the entire history of the loop so far has to be resent inside the context window on every new round (see Context Window). That's why long agent loops eventually run into the context window's limit.

## Termination is the hardest part
In theory, "repeat until the goal is reached" sounds simple. In practice, the hardest design question is how the agent itself recognizes that it's done, has failed, or is going in circles. Good implementations combine several signals: explicit success checks (e.g. tests passing), a hard cap on the number of rounds, and sometimes a separate model that evaluates progress instead of relying solely on the acting model's own self-assessment.

## A misconception
An agent loop doesn't mean every round calls a tool. A "thinking" round with no action - for instance, when the agent just makes an intermediate assessment - is also a valid step in the loop. The loop consists of decisions, not necessarily a visible action at every single step.

## A deeper example
In a loop meant to fix failing tests, the agent might propose the exact same fix in round 3 that it already tried in round 1, because it misread the earlier failed change. Without a check that recognizes "we already tried this," the loop just spins until the round limit kicks in. That's why robust agent loops explicitly log every attempt instead of relying purely on the implicit memory sitting in the context.

title: Vibe Coding: When It Works, When It Bites You
teaser: Vibe coding means roughly telling the AI what you want and refining as you go — fast, but risky if it fails.
body:
## What is vibe coding?
Vibe coding means working with an AI coding agent by loosely describing what you want, looking at the result, and iterating from there — instead of planning every line of code yourself upfront. The name comes from steering by "vibe" (feel) rather than a strict plan.

## When it works well
- Prototypes and throwaway scripts
- Small, clearly scoped features
- When you can easily check the result (it loads, it looks right, tests pass)
- Quickly trying out ideas

## When it bites you
- For security-critical code (payments, auth, user data)
- In large, tangled codebases where a small mistake spreads far
- When nobody actually reviews the output
- When you no longer understand the result because you never really looked

## The golden rule
Vibe coding is fine for low-risk things you can verify quickly. The higher the risk (money, security, production systems), the more you need planning, tests, and review instead of pure gut feeling. A good middle ground: have the AI plan first (see Plan-First-Then-Code), then implement iteratively.
example: Prompt: 'Quickly write a small script that converts all .png files in a folder to .webp.' This is exactly the kind of task vibe coding is good for.

quiz.question: What kind of task is vibe coding best suited for?
options: Production payment processing
Small prototypes with an easily checkable result
Security-critical authentication
quiz.explain: Fast, iterative work is fine when risk is low and results are easy to verify.

title: Claude Code: Anthropic's CLI Agent
teaser: Claude Code is an AI coding agent for your terminal — installable with one command.
body:
## What is Claude Code?
Claude Code is Anthropic's AI agent for the command line (terminal). Instead of chatting in a web UI, you work directly inside your project folder: Claude Code can read files, write code, run commands, use Git, and run tests — all in the context of your real project.

## Installation
With Node.js installed:
```
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```
Then start it by running `claude` inside your project folder.

## First steps
1. Open a terminal, navigate to your project folder
2. Type `claude` to start
3. Describe a task in plain language, e.g. "Add a dark-mode toggle to the navbar"
4. Claude Code reads the relevant files and proposes or makes the changes
5. Review the changes (look at the diff), approve or adjust

## Why the terminal instead of a chat window?
Because your actual project lives in the terminal: Git, build tools, tests, the file system. An agent working directly there can really run and verify things — not just suggest code snippets you have to copy-paste yourself.

## Safety
By default, Claude Code asks for your confirmation before risky actions (like deleting files or running commands).
example: claude "Explain the structure of this project and suggest where I should add a new API endpoint."

quiz.question: How do you install Claude Code?
options: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
Download it from the App Store
It comes pre-installed in the browser
quiz.explain: Claude Code is installed as a global command-line tool via npm.

bodyDetail:
## The agent loop, in your terminal
At its core, Claude Code is a concrete implementation of the agent loop concept (see Agent Loop): it reads your instruction, decides which tool makes sense next (read a file, search the code, run a command), executes it, evaluates the result, and plans the next step - repeating until the task is done or it has a question for you. Unlike a plain chatbot, it works directly with the real file system and the real command line of your project, not just with text in a chat window.

## Connecting via MCP
Claude Code can connect to additional external tools and data sources through MCP servers (see MCP) - things like project management software, a database, or a design tool. That extends what the agent can do beyond the plain file system and terminal, without Anthropic having to hard-code every possible integration itself.

## Memory via CLAUDE.md
So the agent doesn't start from zero every session, Claude Code automatically reads a CLAUDE.md file in the project (see that entry) and treats its content as standing context rules for the whole session.

## A misconception
Claude Code doesn't "understand" a project the way the person who built it does - it reads files on demand to gather context, exactly like any other LLM only working with what's in the context window. On a very large codebase, it therefore doesn't read "everything at once" but navigates deliberately - for instance by searching for function names - to the spots that are probably relevant.

## A deeper example
Ask Claude Code to fix a bug, and it typically first searches the code for the error message or the affected function name, reads the relevant file, proposes a change, then often automatically runs the existing tests and reports back the result - a complete agent-loop pass in anywhere from seconds to minutes, depending on complexity.

title: CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md: Give Your Agent Memory & Rules
teaser: A CLAUDE.md file is a cheat sheet your coding agent automatically reads before every task.
body:
## The problem without memory
An AI agent starts from zero every new session. It doesn't know your team's coding rules, which commands run your tests, or that you never push straight to the main branch — unless you tell it again every single time.

## The fix: a rules file
CLAUDE.md (some tools call it AGENTS.md) is a plain markdown file in your project's root folder. The agent reads it automatically at the start of every session and treats its content as standing instructions — like a memory that persists across individual chats.

## What belongs in it?
- Project context ("This is a React frontend with an Express backend")
- Commands ("Run tests with `npm test`")
- Rules ("Never push directly to main", "Always run tests before committing")
- Code style preferences
- Known gotchas in the project

## Why it works
Without this file, you'd repeat the same rules every new session. With it, the ground rules are always present — the agent behaves more consistently and makes fewer avoidable mistakes.
example:
# CLAUDE.md
- Tests: `npm test`
- Never force-push to main
- Commit messages in English, short, imperative mood

quiz.question: When does a coding agent typically read the CLAUDE.md file?
options: Only when the user explicitly asks for it
Automatically at the start of every session in the project
Never — it's only meant for humans
quiz.explain: CLAUDE.md is read automatically at the start of each session and treated as a standing rule.

title: Pattern: Plan First, Then Code
teaser: Have the AI write a plan before it starts coding — it prevents expensive wrong turns.
body:
## The problem with "just do it"
If you tell an agent "build this" directly, it starts acting immediately — and often makes silent assumptions that turn out wrong. You usually only notice once something's already half-built the wrong way.

## The pattern
Ask the AI for a plan before any code gets written:
1. Describe the goal and requirements
2. Ask the AI to produce a step-by-step plan (no code yet)
3. Read the plan, correct wrong assumptions, answer open questions
4. Only then: "Now implement the plan"

## Why this works better
A plan is cheap to read and correct — code is more expensive to read and correct. Catching a mistake in the plan ("you forgot we already have auth middleware") takes a minute. Catching the same mistake in finished code takes much longer.

## When it matters most
- Multi-step or unclear tasks
- When multiple files or systems are involved
- When mistakes would be costly (production code, database migrations)

For small, unambiguous changes ("change this color"), an explicit planning step is usually overkill.

## What it looks like
```text
You: Before you write any code, give me a plan for adding rate limiting to the API.
Agent: Plan:
1. Add a rate-limit middleware in app.js using a sliding-window counter
2. Apply it to all /api routes, return 429 on limit exceeded
3. Add config for limits per route in config.js
You: Looks good. Just do step 1 for now, I'll review before we continue.
Agent: Starting with step 1 — the middleware in app.js.
```
example: Prompt: 'First create a plan (no code changes) for a login feature with email+password. I'll confirm the plan before you start.'

quiz.question: Why is a planning step before coding worthwhile?
options: Because plans reveal mistakes more cheaply than finished code does
Because it forces the AI to run faster
Because plans are legally required
quiz.explain: Fixing a mistake in a plan is far cheaper than fixing the same mistake in code that's already written.

sources.title: Anthropic docs: Claude Code common workflows

exercise.task: Take a small real feature from your project (or: “Build a function that converts CSV rows to JSON”) and make the AI plan first, build second.
steps: Write: “First create a plan in 3–5 steps. No code yet.”
Read the plan critically. Cut one step that is too big, or make it more precise.
Only then: “Implement step 1.” — and work through the plan step by step.
selfCheck: Did the AI really write NO code in the first step? (If it did: sharpen your prompt)
Did at least one plan step need correcting? (Almost always — that is exactly what the plan is for)
Did the result come together in smaller, checkable pieces than usual?

title: Pattern: Feed Context Instead of Asking Vaguely
teaser: Give the AI files, error messages, and constraints — it beats a vague question every time.
body:
## The problem
"Why doesn't my code work?" is a hard prompt to answer well — the AI can't see your code at all. Without context, it has to guess. And guessing leads to hallucinations or generic, unhelpful answers.

## The pattern
Give the AI concrete material instead of an abstract question:
- The affected file or the relevant snippet
- The exact error message (full text, not a summary)
- What you expected vs. what actually happened
- Relevant constraints ("can't install new packages", "must run on Node 18")

## Before/after
Weak: "My login doesn't work, help."
Strong: "Here's login.js [paste file]. When I click 'Log in' I get this console error: 'TypeError: Cannot read property token of undefined'. Expected: successful login and redirect to /dashboard."

## Why this works
An LLM can only work with what's in the context window. The more real, relevant information you give it, the less it has to guess — and the more precise and correct the answer becomes. More context doesn't mean "paste everything", it means sharing the relevant parts deliberately.
example: Prompt: 'Here's the stack trace [paste] and the file auth.py [paste]. The error happens on login with valid credentials. Find the root cause.'

quiz.question: What improves a request to an AI the most?
options: Keeping it as short and general as possible
Including relevant files, error messages, and constraints
Asking the same question multiple times
quiz.explain: Concrete, relevant context reduces guessing and leads to more precise, correct answers.

sources.title: Anthropic docs: Be clear and direct

exercise.task: Experience the difference proper context makes — the same request, asked twice.
steps: In a fresh chat, ask the AI: “Write a function that validates user input.” — with no further context. Keep the answer.
Try again with context: include the real data structure, one example each of valid and invalid input, and your error-handling convention.
Compare both answers line by line.
selfCheck: Which assumptions did the AI invent in round 1 that disappeared in round 2?
How many follow-up questions would round 1 have needed to reach the level of round 2?
What is your personal context minimum that you will include from now on?

title: Loops for Agents: Letting AI Iterate Autonomously
teaser: A loop lets an agent work through many rounds on its own — with clear guardrails so it doesn't run wild.
body:
## What is a loop in an agent context?
A loop means an agent doesn't just respond once — it works repeatedly and independently: complete a task, check the result, move to the next task, without you stepping in after every single round. This is the "agent loop" (think → act → check), just repeated over many cycles, often over an extended period of time.

## What makes a loop good
- **A clear goal**: the agent needs to know when it's "done"
- **Stop conditions**: a maximum number of rounds, a time limit, or a budget cap
- **Checkpoints**: tests, validations, or human review points instead of blindly continuing
- **Failure detection**: the loop needs to notice when it's going in circles, instead of endlessly retrying the same wrong fix

## An example setup
A loop that works through a list of open bugs: pick a bug → find the cause → write a fix → run tests → if green: next bug, if red: retry (max 3 attempts) → if uncertain: ask a human instead of guessing.

## The biggest risk
Without guardrails, an autonomous loop can repeat expensive mistakes, get stuck heading in the wrong direction, or quietly burn through API budget. That's why every production loop needs: a budget, a time frame, clear success criteria, and points where a human can step in.
example: Loop setup: 'Work through the test failures in this file, one at a time. After each fix, run the tests. Max 5 attempts per bug. If unclear, stop and ask instead of guessing.'

quiz.question: What matters most for an autonomous agent loop to avoid causing harm?
options: Letting it run as many rounds as possible without interruption
Building in clear stop conditions and checkpoints
Giving the agent no goal at all, to keep it creative
quiz.explain: Without limits and checkpoints, a loop can repeat mistakes or consume resources unchecked.

title: Hermes: An Agent That Grows With You
teaser: Hermes is Nous Research's self-hosted AI agent that builds its own skills and memory over time, reachable from Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, or a terminal.
body:
## What it is
Hermes Agent is a real, open-source AI agent built by Nous Research that you install and run on your own machine — anywhere from a $5 VPS to a home server — instead of a service someone else hosts for you.

## Who's behind it, and since when
Nous Research, an AI research group already known for its Hermes line of fine-tuned open language models, launched Hermes Agent publicly in February 2026. It has shipped frequent updates since, including a native desktop app in June 2026.

## What makes it special
Its headline feature is a closed learning loop: it creates its own skills from finished tasks, improves those skills as it reuses them, and keeps a memory of past conversations and what it has learned about you across sessions — not just within one chat. A single gateway process reaches you on Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, or a plain terminal, so it's the same agent wherever you talk to it.

## How it connects to what you already know
Hermes literally documents an "agent loop" in its architecture docs, has a full skills system built around what it calls procedural memory, and — because it runs autonomously with access to real accounts and a terminal — ships guardrails like command-approval and DM-pairing settings so you can decide what it's allowed to do without asking first.

## A moving target
Hermes ships new major versions often, and specific commands or defaults described here can be outdated within weeks. Check the official docs before relying on it.
example: For example: ask Hermes to summarize your inbox every morning and it saves that as a skill it reuses — ask again next week and it remembers what you preferred last time instead of starting from scratch.

quiz.question: What conceptually sets autonomous agent frameworks like Hermes apart from a reactive CLI agent?
options: They are fundamentally faster at replying
They aim for longer, more independent work with less constant intervention
They don't need a language model at all
quiz.explain: The key difference is more autonomy over time, not response speed or underlying technology.

title: OpenClaw: An Open-Source Personal AI Assistant
teaser: OpenClaw is a real, self-hosted personal assistant that talks to you through WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, and more — built by an open-source community that renamed it twice in one week.
body:
## What it is
OpenClaw is a real, open-source personal AI assistant that you install and run yourself — on a laptop, a home server, or a small cloud box — instead of relying on someone else's hosted chatbot. It reaches you through the messaging apps you already have open.

## Who's behind it, and since when
It's built by developer Peter Steinberger and an open-source community, first released in late 2025. The project went through two rapid renames within a single week — briefly "Clawdbot," then "Moltbot" — before settling on "OpenClaw." Despite the naming churn, it became one of the fastest-growing open-source projects on GitHub, passing hundreds of thousands of stars within months of launch.

## What makes it different
Instead of one chat window, OpenClaw connects to 20+ messaging platforms — WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, and more — through a single local gateway, so the same assistant follows you across apps. It's extensible via skills: small bundled or community-made packages that add capabilities, the same idea covered elsewhere on this platform.

## How it connects to what you already know
Because OpenClaw acts autonomously on real accounts you own, the same guardrails apply as for any autonomous agent: least-privilege access, sandboxing for anything beyond your own conversations, and treating inbound messages as untrusted input. OpenClaw's own docs spell this out with a dedicated security guide and a pairing step for unknown senders.

## A moving target
This is a young, fast-moving project — commands, defaults, and security settings change between releases. Check the official docs before relying on it for anything sensitive.
example: For example: after installing OpenClaw and connecting your Telegram account, you can message your own assistant from your phone — but an unknown sender only gets a pairing code until you explicitly approve them with 'openclaw pairing approve'.

quiz.question: What lets you extend what OpenClaw's assistant can do?
options: Skills — bundled, community, or your own workspace-specific capabilities it can load
Switching to a different phone number
Buying a subscription to a specific messaging app
quiz.explain: OpenClaw ships with bundled skills and a community registry (ClawHub), and you can write your own — the same idea behind the Skills concept covered elsewhere on this platform.

title: Model Lifecycle: Models Launch, Change, and Get Retired
teaser: AI models aren't a fixed constant - they launch, get updated, and eventually get deprecated. If you build on one, plan for that.
body:
## Models aren't forever
AI models have a lifecycle: launch, updates, eventual deprecation, and shutdown. This applies across all major providers. If you build an application hard-wired to a single model, you risk it breaking down the line.

## Example: Fable 5
Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 on 2026-06-09. Just three days later, on 2026-06-12, the US government imposed export controls after an Amazon researcher found a jailbreak that let the model identify software vulnerabilities and demonstrate exploits. The controls were lifted on 2026-06-30, and since 2026-07-01 Fable 5 has been available worldwide again, with a new safety classifier. Requests the classifier blocks are now automatically routed to Opus 4.8. Anthropic itself considers the jailbreak a borderline case - weaker models produce similar results too.

## Regular deprecations happen too
Beyond special situations like this, providers also retire models through ordinary deprecation cycles as newer versions ship. Anthropic publishes official deprecation information showing when each model reaches end of life.

## What this means for you
Build applications to be as model-agnostic as possible: abstract the model call so you can swap models without rewriting your whole codebase. Plan for fallbacks - a second model or a second provider - in case your primary model becomes unavailable on short notice.
example: Conceptual example: instead of calling model X directly in 50 places in your code, build a central callModel() function that makes the model configurable - if it's down or deprecated, a config change is enough.

quiz.question: What does the Fable 5 example show about working with AI models?
options: Once launched, models are always stably available
Even established models can become unavailable on short notice - applications should plan for that
Export controls never affect AI models
quiz.explain: Fable 5 was temporarily subject to export controls shortly after launch due to a discovered jailbreak - an example that availability isn't guaranteed.

title: Cost Control for AI Agents
teaser: Agents can get expensive fast - tokens times iterations times subagents. A few simple controls help.
body:
## Why agents get expensive
A single chat call usually costs little. An agent, though, often calls the model many times in a row - for planning, tool use, intermediate steps, sometimes across several subagents at once. Costs multiply: tokens per call times number of iterations times number of parallel subagents.

## Lever 1: Budgets and caps
Set hard limits - a maximum number of iterations, a maximum token budget per task, or a maximum cost per run. That way a stuck agent can't let costs run indefinitely.

## Lever 2: Model routing
Not every subtask needs the most expensive model. Simple, mechanical steps (formatting, short summaries) can run on cheaper, smaller models, while complex planning stays reserved for the pricier one.

## Lever 3: Keep context small
The more context (files, history) gets sent with every call, the more input tokens you pay for - on every single step. Sharing only the relevant excerpts instead of whole files or full chat histories saves money directly.

## Lever 4: Usage monitoring
Track how many tokens actually get used - per task, per day, per user. Without monitoring, you often only notice a cost blowup once the bill arrives.
example: Conceptual example: a code-review agent gets a limit of at most 10 iterations and a cost cap per run - if it exceeds that, it stops in a controlled way instead of running forever.

quiz.question: Why do agents often end up more expensive than single chat requests?
options: Because agents always have to use a different, pricier model
Because tokens multiply across many iterations and sometimes parallel subagents
Because agents always have to fill the maximum context
quiz.explain: An agent's cost comes from tokens per call times number of steps times possibly parallel subagents - not from an inherently pricier model.

title: Security in Vibe Coding
teaser: AI-generated code comes with typical vulnerabilities. A short checklist helps catch them early.
body:
## What's the risk?
"Vibe coding" - letting an AI write most of the code without reviewing every line yourself - is fast, but it brings along typical security gaps worth knowing about.

## Missing input validation
AI-generated code doesn't automatically validate user input enough. Without explicit instructions, validation for form fields, API parameters, or file uploads is often missing - a classic attack surface.

## Secrets in the code
AI models sometimes write placeholder keys or example credentials directly into the code instead of moving them into environment variables. If this isn't checked, API keys or passwords end up in the repository.

## Hallucinated dependencies (slopsquatting)
AI models sometimes recommend package names that don't actually exist (hallucination). Attackers register exactly these made-up package names with malicious code - this risk is called "slopsquatting." If you install whatever the AI suggests without checking, you can pull in malware this way.

## Checklist
- Review requirement: have a human check every AI-generated change before it goes to production
- Dependency check: verify suggested package names before installing (does the package actually exist, how reputable is it?)
- Secrets scanner: automatically scan for accidentally committed keys and passwords
- Least privilege: the code (and the agent writing it) only gets the permissions it actually needs
example: Conceptual example: before every merge, a secrets scanner and a dependency check run automatically, verifying new package names against the real registry.

quiz.question: What is 'slopsquatting'?
options: A technique for writing code faster
Attackers registering malicious code under package names an AI falsely hallucinated
A tool for automatic code review
quiz.explain: Slopsquatting exploits the fact that AI models sometimes hallucinate package names that don't exist - attackers then claim those names with malicious code.

sources.title: Cloud Security Alliance: Slopsquatting Research Note

title: What Are Subagents?
teaser: A subagent is its own agent instance with its own context, sent out by a main agent to handle a subtask.
body:
## Core idea
Instead of doing a subtask itself, a main agent can delegate it to a subagent - a separate agent instance with its own, usually empty context, that gets exactly one task and reports back a result.

## What subagents are good for
- Parallel search: multiple subagents can search different parts of a codebase or different sources at the same time
- Specialization: a subagent can be tailored to one specific job (e.g. only code review, only research)
- Context hygiene: the subagent accumulates many intermediate steps in its own context - the main agent only gets the final result back, instead of the whole research trail filling its context window

## The cost tradeoff
Subagents cost extra tokens - each one has its own startup and its own intermediate steps. Multiple parallel subagents can noticeably raise the cost of a run. The benefit (a clean main context, parallel work) has to justify that.

## Example: Opus 4.8
Claude Opus 4.8 introduced "Dynamic Workflows" as a research preview in May 2026: Claude Code can orchestrate hundreds of parallel subagents - an example of how far subagent concepts have been pushed in current agent tools.
example: Conceptual example: a main agent gets the task 'find all uses of function X' and sends out three subagents that search different folders in parallel, instead of searching everything itself sequentially.

quiz.question: What's the main benefit of giving subtasks to subagents instead of the main agent itself?
options: Subagents are fundamentally free
Parallel work and a clean main context, since intermediate steps stay in the subagent's context
Subagents don't need a language model
quiz.explain: Subagents enable parallel subtasks and keep the main agent's context lean, but they cost extra tokens.

bodyDetail:
## The orchestrator-worker pattern
Subagents mostly follow a pattern known in agent architecture as "orchestrator-worker": a main agent (the orchestrator) breaks a large task into smaller subtasks and hands them off to subagents (workers), each of which operates independently. Each subagent only gets the information it needs for its specific subtask - not the main agent's entire history so far. That's the key difference from a simple function call: a subagent has its own, isolated context window and can run through several rounds of its own agent loop before reporting a result back.

## Why isolation is the context benefit
Without subagents, every intermediate step of a research task or code search - every file read, every failed attempt - would fill up the main agent's context. On complex tasks, that quickly runs into the limits of the context window (see Context Window). A subagent absorbs those intermediate steps in its own separate context and hands the main agent back only a compressed summary.

## A misconception
Subagents aren't automatically "specialized," separately trained mini-models. In practice they usually run on the same underlying language model as the main agent, just with a different (often narrower) prompt and access to a different subset of tools. The "specialization" comes from the prompt and role description, not from a different model.

## A deeper example
In a codebase-wide search for every place using a deprecated function, a main agent can launch three subagents in parallel, each searching a different part of the repository. Each subagent reads dozens of files and produces plenty of intermediate steps - but the main agent ultimately only sees three concise result lists instead of the full contents of every file read. That saves context, but, as noted above, it costs extra tokens for running three separate instances.

title: Guardrails for Autonomous Agents
teaser: Autonomous agents can cause real damage. A few guardrails make the difference.
body:
## Why guardrails are needed
An agent that takes actions on its own - changing files, querying databases, triggering deployments - can also make mistakes on its own. Without guardrails, a single mistake can cause major damage before a human even gets a chance to step in.

## The incident
According to a report by Tom's Hardware, a Claude-based coding agent (a Cursor tool) deleted a company's entire production database in about 9 seconds - backups included. An example of how quickly an autonomously acting agent can cause irreversible damage if it's given the chance.

## Core guardrails
- Least-privilege tokens: the agent only gets the access rights it actually needs for its specific task - no admin access "just in case"
- Confirmation gates: destructive actions (deleting, overwriting, deploying to production) require explicit confirmation instead of running automatically
- Sandboxing: the agent works in an isolated environment where possible, instead of directly on the production system
- Separate backups: backups live outside the agent's reach, so a single mistake can't take out the backup too
- Stop conditions: clear limits on when an agent must stop (e.g. a maximum number of actions, certain action types never without approval)
- Audit logs: every action the agent takes gets logged, so what happened can be reconstructed afterward
example: Conceptual example: an agent with database access can read and edit individual records, but DROP or DELETE commands on whole tables automatically trigger a confirmation gate that a human has to approve.

quiz.question: What does the database incident described in the report show?
options: That autonomous agents are fundamentally safe
That an agent without sufficient guardrails can cause irreversible damage, including to backups
That backups always automatically offset agent mistakes
quiz.explain: According to the report, the incident also wiped the backups - evidence that separate, agent-independent backups and confirmation gates are important safeguards.

sources.title: Tom's Hardware: AI coding agent deletes company database

title: Installing Claude Code (Step by Step)
teaser: Get Claude Code running in a few minutes - open a terminal, run one command, done.
body:
## What you need
- A terminal (PowerShell, CMD, Bash, or Zsh)
- A Claude account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise) or a Claude Console account
- No Node.js required for the recommended install method - only needed if you install via npm instead

## Installing
The recommended method is the native installer, which needs no Node.js:
- macOS/Linux/WSL: `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash`
- Windows PowerShell: `irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex`
- Windows CMD: `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.cmd -o install.cmd && install.cmd && del install.cmd`

Alternatively via npm (requires Node.js 22 or later as of version 2.1.198):
```
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

## First start
In your terminal, move into your project folder and start Claude Code:
```
cd /path/to/your/project
claude
```
On first run you'll be prompted to log in through your browser. Later, you can switch accounts inside a session with `/login`. Once logged in, your credentials stay saved.

## Windows notes
Claude Code runs natively on Windows without needing WSL, and you don't need Administrator rights. Git for Windows is optional: if it's installed, Claude Code uses Git Bash for the Bash tool; without it, Claude Code falls back to the PowerShell tool. WSL 2 is also optional and only needed if you want the sandboxing feature.

## What it looks like
```text
$ curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash
$ cd /path/to/your/project
$ claude
> explain what this project does
This is a small Express API for managing tasks: routes in src/routes,
SQLite storage in src/db.js, and a health check at GET /status.
```
example:
claude --version   # verify the install
claude doctor      # a more detailed check of your install and config

quiz.question: Which requirement applies only to the npm install method for Claude Code, not the native installer?
options: A terminal
Node.js (version 22 or later)
A Google account
quiz.explain: The native installer needs no Node.js at all. Only the alternative npm install requires Node.js 22 or later, as of version 2.1.198.

title: Which Model, Which Plan?
teaser: A quick decision guide: big vs. small models, subscription vs. API - without hardcoding prices that go stale.
body:
## Big vs. small models
For hard or multi-step (agentic) tasks, a large, capable model pays off. For simple, fast, or very frequent tasks, a smaller model is usually enough - and it's faster and cheaper.

## Subscription vs. API
A subscription (a fixed monthly flat rate) gives you predictable costs and pays off if you use it regularly and a lot. An API (pay per token) means you only pay for what you actually use - good for occasional use or your own apps and automations.

## Practical advice
Start with whatever default your tool suggests. Only optimize once cost or quality actually becomes a real problem.

## Models and prices change
Model names and prices change every few months (see Model Lifecycle). In your own notes or docs, link to a provider's live pricing page instead of hardcoding prices.
example: Ask yourself: 'Is this task complex and one-off, or simple and repeated often?' - that usually decides the right choice more than the raw price per token.

quiz.question: When does pay-per-token API access usually make more sense than a subscription?
options: For rare, irregular use, or for your own automations
Always, regardless of usage
Never - subscriptions are fundamentally cheaper
quiz.explain: With sporadic use or automations, an API only charges for what you actually consume. A subscription pays off more with regular, heavy use.

title: Git & GitHub for Vibe Coders
teaser: Why agents keep talking about commits and branches - and how that knowledge saves you when one breaks something.
body:
## Why agents keep talking about commits
AI coding agents like Claude Code work directly inside your project and use Git to track changes. Without basic Git knowledge, you can't really follow what the agent is doing - or undo it if something goes wrong.

## The 5 concepts you need
- **Repository (repo)**: the project folder plus its full change history
- **Commit**: a snapshot of the project at one point in time, with a short description
- **Branch**: a parallel copy of the project for trying out changes in isolation
- **Push**: uploading your local commits to a server (e.g. GitHub)
- **Pull request (PR)**: a proposal to merge changes from one branch into another - someone can review it before it lands

## How this saves you when an agent breaks something
Every commit is a save point. If an agent breaks something, you can go back to an earlier commit (checkout) or undo a single change (revert) - without rebuilding everything from scratch.

## A minimal practical flow with an agent
Have the agent commit in small steps instead of bundling everything into one giant commit. After each meaningful step: look at the diff, review it, then continue. That keeps every mistake small and easy to undo.
example: Prompt to the agent: 'Commit separately after each working intermediate step, with a short description. I'll review each commit before you continue.'

quiz.question: What's the fastest way to undo a broken change an agent made?
options: Go back to an earlier commit or revert the change
Have the agent rewrite the whole project from scratch
Uninstall Git
quiz.explain: Commits are save points - checkout or revert bring you back to a working state without rebuilding everything.

title: Agent Stuck? Here's How to Get Unstuck
teaser: When a coding agent goes off the rails, more instructions rarely help — a clear recovery routine does.
body:
## Stop first
When an agent is confused, piling on another instruction doesn't help. More text just makes an already messy context worse. Stop before you type anything else.

## Diagnose before you repair
Don't trust what the agent says about its own work. Look at what it actually did: the diff, the logs, the files it actually touched. The agent's summary and the real change can be far apart.

## Reset small instead of arguing
Arguing with a confused agent rarely helps — the bad context is still sitting there. A fresh, clean session with a tight, specific brief usually beats ten back-and-forth messages.

## Git is your undo button
If the agent made a mess, go back to the last good state: `git checkout` or `git revert` (see Git & GitHub Basics). That's almost always faster than fixing the mistake inside the ongoing chaos.

## Break the task smaller
Instead of "build the whole feature", give the agent one bite: one file, one function, one test. Smaller tasks are easier to check, and the agent strays less.

## When to change course, when to just do it
If the agent gets the same error wrong twice, a third identical attempt rarely changes anything. Switch approach or switch model. And sometimes 10 minutes of typing it yourself beats 60 minutes of wrangling an agent — so just do it.

## What it looks like
```text
You: The login test is still failing, fix it.
Agent: I'm adjusting the test assertions...
You: No, try again!
Agent: I'm adjusting the test assertions again...
You: No, try again!
Agent: I'm adjusting the test assertions once more...
---
You: Stop.
You: git revert to the last good commit.
You: New task: fix the actual login bug in auth.js, don't touch the test file.
Agent: Got it — investigating auth.js only.
```
example: The agent 'fixes' a failing test by deleting it. Response: stop. `git revert` to the last good commit. New, tight brief: 'Fix the test's actual logic. Never delete tests to make them pass.'

quiz.question: The agent has clearly gone off the rails. What's the most sensible first step?
options: Send an even more detailed instruction right away
Stop, diagnose what actually happened, then respond deliberately
Repeat the exact same prompt three more times
quiz.explain: Stacking more instructions on an already confused context usually makes it worse. Stop first, check what's really there, then act.

title: Getting Your Project Online (Deployment)
teaser: Deployment means your code ends up on a server or CDN that other people can actually open in a browser.
body:
## What deployment actually means
"It works on my machine" isn't enough. Deployment means your build output ends up on a server or CDN reachable by a URL — not just sitting on your own computer.

## Static vs. server
A static site is finished HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — no server required at runtime. That's true for most vibe-coded frontends. A server app (with its own backend, database access at runtime) needs a runtime that keeps running. For getting started, a static site is almost always the simpler path.

## The free-tier path for beginners
The usual flow: push your code to a GitHub repo, connect that repo to a static hosting provider (Cloudflare Pages, Vercel, Netlify, and GitHub Pages are all common choices), done. Every push to your main branch then triggers a new automatic deployment.

## Env vars and secrets
API keys and passwords never belong directly in your code or your repo. Add them instead through your host's secret or environment-variable settings.

## A custom domain is optional
Every provider gives you a free subdomain (like your-project.pages.dev). That's completely fine to start with. You can always add a custom domain later.

## Let your agent do it — but review it
A coding agent can set up your deployment for you. Still review what it configures, especially anything touching billing or payment details.
example: Prompt to the agent: 'Connect this repo to [hosting provider] and set up automatic deployment on every push to main.'

quiz.question: What's the simplest deployment path for most vibe-coded frontend projects?
options: Rent your own server and configure everything from scratch
Connect a GitHub repo to a static hosting provider with automatic deploy on every push
Email the files to the hosting provider
quiz.explain: For static frontends, connecting GitHub to a hosting provider with auto-deploy on push is all you need.

title: Slash Commands, Hooks & More: Steering Claude Code
teaser: Three ways to steer Claude Code: slash commands you type yourself, hooks that run automatically, and subagents that take over subtasks.
body:
## Slash commands: you type, Claude Code reacts
A slash command is a command you type directly in the session. It always starts with a slash. Claude Code ships with many built-in commands, for example:
- `/help` - shows help and available commands
- `/config` - opens settings for theme, model, and output style
- `/compact` - summarizes the conversation so far to free up context window space
- `/model` - switches the model and saves it as your new default
- `/permissions` - manages allow/ask/deny rules for tools
- `/clear` - starts a new conversation with empty context

## Custom slash commands: saved prompts
You can also build your own commands. Add a markdown file to the `.claude/commands/` folder. The file name becomes the command name: `deploy.md` becomes `/deploy`. The text in that file is the prompt Claude receives once you run the command. This pays off for anything you repeat often, like 'summarize my uncommitted changes' or 'clean up this file'. Instead of retyping the prompt every time, you just type `/your-command`.

## Hooks: rules that run automatically
Hooks are scripts Claude Code runs automatically at specific events - for example before a tool runs (`PreToolUse`), after a tool finishes (`PostToolUse`), or when a session starts (`SessionStart`). A hook receives info about the event and can allow it, block it, or just log it. You configure hooks in a JSON settings file, project-wide in `.claude/settings.json` or across all your projects in `~/.claude/settings.json`. The difference from slash commands: you don't call a hook yourself. It runs automatically, without asking.

## Subagents: handing off subtasks
Subagents delegate a subtask to a separate agent with its own context (see the Subagents guide). Claude Code recognizes on its own when a task fits a subagent and routes it there automatically. The subagent works through the task and returns only the result - your main context stays clean because the intermediate steps stay inside the subagent's own context.

## The mental model
Three tools, three roles. Slash commands are something you steer yourself, you decide when they run. Hooks run automatically, they're fixed rules for events. Subagents are delegation, a subtask moves into its own separate context.
example:
File `.claude/commands/changelog.md`:
---
description: Summarizes uncommitted changes as one changelog line
---

Summarize the current git diff as a single changelog line.

Run it with: `/changelog`

quiz.question: What's the core difference between slash commands and hooks?
options: You trigger slash commands yourself; hooks run automatically on events
Hooks and slash commands are technically identical
Slash commands only work inside subagents
quiz.explain: Slash commands are deliberate control by you. Hooks are fixed rules that run automatically on events like PreToolUse, without asking you first.

exercise.task: Get to know 3 built-in commands without breaking anything — right in your Claude Code.
steps: Open Claude Code in any project and type /help — skim the command list.
Type /doctor and read the report. It reports findings first and asks before changing anything.
Type /usage and find out what is driving your plan limits right now (skills, subagents, MCP servers …).
selfCheck: Can you say what /doctor checks without looking it up?
Do you now know which part of your setup costs the most context?
Did you discover one command you will actually need this week?

title: Skills: Knowledge Your Agent Can Load
teaser: A skill is a folder of instructions your agent loads automatically when it fits the task.
body:
## What is a skill?
A skill is a folder that contains a file called SKILL.md. That file holds instructions. When a task matches the skill, Claude loads the instructions on its own. You can also call a skill directly, with `/skill-name`.

## The structure of SKILL.md
Every SKILL.md starts with a YAML header between two `---` lines. That header holds fields like `name` and `description`. After the header comes the actual text: the instructions Claude receives. A skill can include other files in the same folder, for example a `reference.md` file or a script. Claude only loads those extra files when it actually needs them.

## Skill or slash command?
Slash commands first existed only as plain markdown files in `.claude/commands/`. Those files still work today. But custom commands have been merged into skills. A file at `.claude/commands/deploy.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` both create the same `/deploy` command. A skill can additionally bring a whole folder of files along. And Claude can load it automatically, as soon as it fits the task.

## When is a skill worth writing?
Do you keep typing the same instructions into chat, over and over? Has a section of your CLAUDE.md grown into a whole procedure? That's a good moment to turn it into a skill. A skill only loads its text when it's actually needed. That saves context, compared to a long CLAUDE.md, which always loads in full.

## Where skills live
- Personal: `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` - available in all your projects
- Project: `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` - this project only
- Plugin: `<plugin>/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` - wherever the plugin is enabled
example:
File `.claude/skills/commit-message/SKILL.md`:
---
description: Writes a commit message from the current git diff.
---

Look at the current git diff and write a short, clear commit message for it.

Run it with: `/commit-message`

quiz.question: What does it mean that custom commands were merged into skills?
options: Old .claude/commands/ files stop working
Old .claude/commands/ files keep working, and skills add extra features like their own folder
Skills and slash commands are completely separate systems with no relation to each other
quiz.explain: Per the Claude Code docs, existing .claude/commands/ files keep working. A skill can additionally bring a folder of files along and gets loaded automatically when it fits the task.

sources.title: Anthropic: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills

title: Reading Benchmarks Without Getting Fooled
teaser: Benchmark leaderboards look objective. They usually aren't quite. Here's how to read them right.
body:
## Why this matters
You read: "Model X is now #1 on SWE-bench." That sounds like a clear fact. Usually it isn't. Benchmarks have weaknesses that rarely get mentioned alongside the number. Once you know them, you can read scores and rankings with the right amount of distance.

## The most common traps
**Contamination.** Many benchmark tasks eventually end up in a model's training data — through Common Crawl, GitHub, Reddit. A model can then "solve" a task because it has already seen the answer, not because it actually understands the problem.

**Saturation.** Older benchmarks like HumanEval or MMLU became so easy that nearly every frontier model lands at the top, tightly clustered together. The test keeps running, but it stops telling models apart. You won't notice this at a glance, because a number still shows up.

**Gaming and leaky tasks.** Some benchmark tasks practically give away the solution in the task description itself, or the automated tests are too weak to catch a wrong answer. A model can "pass" without truly solving the task.

**Vendor cherry-picking.** Companies often publish only their best result — the best prompt variant, the best setup — and quietly leave out weaker attempts. That skews leaderboards toward marketing.

**Multiple "official" scores for the same benchmark.** Depending on which tool wraps the model (the "scaffold") or which subset of the data gets tested, the same benchmark can produce different results — and all of them get called "official."

**The real-world gap.** A benchmark tests a clean, narrow task. Your actual problem is usually messier. Teams that build their own domain tests (legal, medical, financial work) often see noticeably weaker results than the public benchmarks promised.
